speaker: interviewer
text: Interview with Mrs. Thea Rumstein. You-- you had mentioned earlier that material things are-- are not necessarily important to you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: --partially as a result of your experience--

speaker: interviewee
text: Definitely.

speaker: interviewer
text: --in the war.

speaker: interviewee
text: Definitely.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is important to you, then?

speaker: interviewee
text: What is important? My family. My closest friends. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] My health. Our health. That's very important, that we stay around for a while and live every day. Live every day. It's easy to say don't worry about later. Don't worry about what was. Live now. And if you want to do something now, do it. Don't let [INAUDIBLE]. Never push it for later. If I feel I have to do something, I do it. I think this was part of it, because you never know what tomorrow brings. Even when you are younger, you don't know what tomorrow brings. So if you're really-- and it has [INAUDIBLE] to do that-- it has to do-- you have to be wealthy to do it. If it's something you want to do, you should pursue it.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mentioned your husband is also a survivor. Was that important to you when-- when the two of you were getting married? Did that--

speaker: interviewee
text: That he was a survivor?

speaker: interviewer
text: That he was a survivor also?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, it wasn't important. But it-- it was our-- we have the same background, you know? I knew his mother. We've become-- we're, like, related. We come from the same background. And we have the same thing in common. And we have the same values. That was very important for a marriage that lasted, so far, 41 years-- [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Right

speaker: interviewee
text: --because sometimes, you know, if this doesn't go together, then it cannot last. So that helped a lot. And of course, grateful that-- that we're still here and that we made it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did the two of you talk a lot about your wartime experiences, or--

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't think so, no. We-- we-- we-- even with our-- with Julia, when she was younger, we didn't say, oh, we didn't have this or that, because even with my grandchildren now, I slowly-- because the oldest one starts learning about the Holocaust. I don't want to burden little children, you know, with-- with-- with the past. Not yet. I want to do it gradually, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: They can-- and once I-- I had my-- my oldest grandson. When he heard about it, he says, does-- really, they-- they killed all the Jews? And why? Only because of they are Jewish? He says, what if they changed their religion? So, you know, it kind of-- if you change your religion, then you're-- you're not going to be persecuted. So I told him it didn't matter under Hitler. You had to have four Christian grandparents in order to be saved. And I don't know how many generations back. It didn't help. Whoever changed their religion, it didn't help them.

speaker: interviewee
text: So-- but like I said, I didn't-- I-- we didn't speak much because we felt it would burden a child or-- or-- later on, of course, she took a Holocaust course and-- and-- and knew about it. Now she knows about everything. And if she sees the tape, she'll know even more--

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: --because like I said, we always felt-- we never wanted to talk about-- even with people who were not in the camp. Very few people we discussed our-- what happened, because-- and I still feel-- I know it is maybe wrong-- if you haven't been there, you don't-- you don't know how it was.

speaker: interviewer
text: I have not seen-- I'm sorry, Mr. Spielberg. I have not seen I didn't want to see it because to me, if I see it, what is it going to tell me? I know how it was. It's a re-enactment. It's for people who have not been there. Means a lot. It's a wonder. I understand it's a wonder. I mean, it won the Academy Award. It's a wonderful film, I'm sure. But I can't-- and the same thing is-- I went to Yard Vashem. But I'm afraid to go to the Holocaust Museum. I don't want to see the cattle cars. I don't want to see the-- the-- the-- all these things that will remind me of how it was. Maybe this is psychologically, I try to-- I can't forget. But I don't want to dwell on it. I don't want to be burdened, you know? That it just sticks in my head all the time. To see it, I know I-- I would get very excited. You mentioned your grandchildren. And they're still very young. You don't want to burden them a lot with-- with this right now.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: What message would you want to leave them on this tape for later?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, first of all, they should remember their grandfather and grandmother, and that they love them very much. And also, they should be proud to be Jewish. They live in Connecticut, a very nice area-- not predominantly Jewish. But my daughter, even though we did not have a religious home-- I made Passover seder. We going to temple on Yom Kippur and Rosh Hashanah. She's joined a synagogue. The kids go to Hebrew school. Brian will be bar mitzvahed-- bar mitzvahed next May 31. And they should remember that they are-- they are Jewish. Should be proud of it. And-- and that's it. Should be just proud being-- having-- having a good background. That's all.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Is there anything else you'd like to add in closing?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I-- I want to thank, you know, you guys. And I want to thank Steven Spielberg again for doing that good work. And-- and I hope it wasn't too long. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Didn't speak too much. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Otherwise, I'm-- I'm glad that you came. And I finally-- I'm glad I made up my mind to talk about it, my past.

speaker: interviewer
text: And we'd like to thank you very much for your testimony.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're welcome.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mrs. Rumstein, would you like to introduce your husband to us, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I'd like to. This is my husband of 41 years. It's going on 42. Jack-- Jack-- we call him Jackie. And what can I tell you about him? He's-- he's a good husband. He was a good husband. And we have a very good marriage for all these years. And--

speaker: interviewee
text: We try to do the best.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, we do the best. And we do a lot of things. And we have common interests. And-- and that's it. I don't know what else to say about him. He's a good guy. Everybody likes him. He's a--

speaker: interviewee
text: I hope so. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewee
text: He jokes-- he jokes a lot. And what do you have to say? You have something to say? [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, she told you the whole story. And we hope that people will recognize for what-- what we went through all these years. And for the future, the children and all of them should know what happened [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in those terrible years--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: --past.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we feel-- we feel we are survivors. We really feel like survivors. And that makes us stronger. It made us stronger over the years, with all the things that happened from '45 to-- to '96. All kinds of things happened. But we survived it. We're still here. Hope for-- for quite some time.

speaker: interviewee
text: Hopefully.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right? [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Thank you both very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you, sir.

speaker: interviewee
text: And you, thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, this is my most precious picture because it is my father, my mother, and me, and my brother. From left to right is my mother. And I'm the little baby on her lap, so it's the day I was-- the-- the year I was born. And on the right side is my father with my brother, who is-- when I'm-- if I'm a baby, he is probably 18 months old there. And it was done in a-- in a little garden, a coffeehouse garden not far where we lived. And I really have that picture because my parents must have sent it either to my aunt in Israel or my aunt in England. And that's how I got it, because that's the only thing we got if we send it out of the country.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your parents' names?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is Gusti. And Jula-- Julius. That's my father.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: And my brother's George on the right side. And I'm the little baby on the left, Thea. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This, again, is a picture that was sent by my parents to her sister-- my mother's sister in Israel. On that left is me. I was six years old. And on the right is my brother, George-- Georg. He-- he was seven. And again, these are precious pre-war pictures, which are very few that I have. So I'm-- I like them. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And this is a picture taken when I was 11 years old. So it was 1939. In front of a swastika because they were all over. And this was a store with a swastika. I guess I couldn't get away from it. With my girlfriend, who was 12 years old. She was a year older than I am. But I'm the taller one. I'm on the right. She's on the left. And she perished. She and her whole family perished during Hitler.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember her name, by any chance?

speaker: interviewee
text: Susie Shanker. Yes, her name was Susie Shanker. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This is my father, Jula-- Julius. And this was taken in 1943 as a kind of passport picture. And he perished in 1945-- '44, excuse me. In October 1944. And I loved him very much. It took me a long time to get over his death.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know where this picture was taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture was taken someplace in Vienna. I really don't know. Maybe it was taken for an identification card, because we had to take-- go around with them. So again, I got this from someplace, from either Israel or from England. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] This is my mother. This was taken approximately in the '50s, maybe '54, '55-- 1954, '55, in Vienna. And she's Gusti. Her name was Gusti. She survived together with me. And she lived to be a nice ripe old age of 86 years old. And I'm very grateful for that. She stayed in Vienna-- she-- all the time. But she came to America every year. And we spent months together. And we again went to Vienna and visited her there too, and was always a wonderful time. I have wonderful memories of her. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] This is an interesting story. Gertie-- my friend Gertie last year went to Austria. And she visited Mauthausen. And as she looked among all the other pictures she took, she saw this picture on the wall. And she recognized my mother, me, and her with these dresses that we made ourselves from these tablecloths that the Nazis, SS left behind. And my mother is carrying that little striped satchel that we all made by hand. We sat around and we made it because there was no other way we could get back out of there because we didn't have any other clothes. And it's coincidental that-- that is us. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] This is a dress that I made. Actually, we all three made it. My mother, my sister, and-- Gertie, and-- and me, when we left-- before we came back from Mauthausen, we had nothing to wear. We just wore men underwear. So we found these tablecloths, which we cut up and we made dresses for us, including that little satchel. And when you look at these dresses and that satchel, you can see on the picture that we showed before that the three of us standing with the same dress and with the same satchel. Nobody else had this. Just us.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. You say the dress was made from tablecloths?

speaker: interviewee
text: From-- what-- we found it. We-- we found it. It might have been a sheet. It might have been-- whatever it was, we-- we used it. We cut it up. And we put-- so we had something to wear now.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the satchel was made from what?

speaker: interviewee
text: The satchel-- we found this. There were jackets there, like-- but they were torn. So we just cut it in pieces and made a little satchel, because at that time, we knew when we went to Vienna, we had pieces of bread. We had something to eat already. So what-- otherwise, we didn't have anything else. So we figured if we get any kind of food, we can carry it here so we have something to eat. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] It's all made by hand. [LAUGHS] The material we found. And I suppose the-- the needle and the thread we got already from our liberators. We asked for that. [PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS] This is my release certification from concentration camp Mauthausen. Without that, we could not have left. And this is all the information, with my fingerprint and how long I was in the camp. And we needed to have that because we had no other papers. So these are our-- it's my-- it was my own certification of my existence at that time, before I-- I came back to Vienna. And we had to show that on every step we went, who we are. [PAUSES FOR 17 SECONDS] This picture was taken-- as you can see, we recuperated already. On the left side is Gertie-- Gertie Taussig Meltzer. On the right side is me. And we-- we're very happy with our clothes, that we had normal clothes. It was taken probably in 1900-- already-- either '46 or '47, because '46-- 1946 in the fall, something like that. And we were very young. I was-- we were 18-year-old girls. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] This is a story that I actually left out before. As I mentioned, when I came to Mauthausen, there was one guard who helped us, helped me-- or at least, he pacified me, because I thought we're going to be gassed. Now, after he brought us something to eat, he disappeared. And the next time I saw him when I was in a gypsy camp. And he came by. He took off his uniform. And when he saw me, he came towards me and he says he's leaving or he's running away. And he asked me for my name and exactly where I came from, and-- which I mentioned before, but he made sure that he remembers and he writes down my name. And he disappeared. And after I came back to Vienna and I started working for the American joint, one day in April 1946, I-- two American soldiers came. They were looking for me. They knew my name. And they went to the-- it's called the Israelitische Kultusgemeinde, where they-- where I was registered. And they were looking for some Thea Guardsman. And they found me. And they told me that I have to appear in Dachau as a witness. Well, at that time, I really didn't know what it meant because I really-- I just didn't know. I was-- but I was young. And for me, it was kind of a-- something fun to do. And actually, it was my first time that I flew in a plane with two-- with those two guys from Vienna to Munich. And from there I was picked up and brought into the Dachau camp, which was now a camp for-- for Nazis. They had Nazis. The only thing is that they played soccer there and they get good food, because the Americans are not the Germans. So they had a better time than they had before. Well, I still didn't know what-- what they wanted. So they-- they'd showed me a picture book, book of-- of photos. And they told me to see if I recognize someone. And I went through a whole bunch of photos until I come to this picture of this specific guy. His name was George-- Georg. And I said, I know this guy. Yes, this is the guy who requested you to come and talk as a witness for the defense. Well, that made me terrible, terrible mad because I felt that, yes, he helped us. It was a few days before the war ended. He probably-- he knew he needed an alibi. And I really didn't know what he did before. But since he was a guard in the camp, he must not have been an angel.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I expressed my-- my dismay. And I said, for that, you picking me up and bringing me here so that I should say something good about the Nazi? And, well, they were very understanding. And I had to write everything about it. And I also had to be before the court. This was the Dachau court. And there were 62 of these guards that they caught. And he was among them. And I saw them. I saw them sitting there.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I told my story, just as I did now, how he helped me just for a short time and just once, with probably ulterior motives. And that's the only thing I can tell. I cannot say that he was a good person or he was a-- was not a Nazi. And this is what I told them. And I also requested to be sent back as soon as possible because I was actually embarrassed to be a witness for the defense instead of a witness for the prosecution. All ended pretty good. I read later in the paper that all 62 were convicted. But I'm sure that they came out very, very soon. And either they still live or they had a life after that without too much persecution, because not too much happened except for the Nuremberg process happened to-- to all these-- rest of the Nazis, because everybody denied to know-- that they knew what happened. And that's the story. I'm standing here. And I had to make my oath that I tell the truth and but-- nothing but the truth. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This is Jack and Thea. I'm Thea on the left, Jack on the right. This was taken just before he went to the United States. He immigrated in 1947, summer '47. This was taken in-- in 1946. And we liked each other a lot. But he left and I stayed back. But we met again seven years later.

speaker: interviewer
text: Like they're sitting there, yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the-- this is our pride. This is our daughter, Julia, in the middle. She is a-- she's an accountant, a CPA. Very smart girl. This is her husband, Bob-- Robert. He's a dentist. On the left side, the tall guy-- now he's even taller-- is Brian, Brian David. He's going to be bar mitzvahed next year. In the middle, underneath Julia, is Alex, the sweet Alex. He's six years old. And on the right side is Gregory. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And he is-- he's going to be nine in-- this month. And they're all wonderful, wonderful children. And so is-- are the grownups. And we are very proud of them.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's it.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is tiresome. Well, I feel better. Hope.
